annotations.text: Pulmonary hypertension may complicate many parenchymal lung diseases, resulting secondarily from obliteration of alveolar capillary membranes or hypoxic vasoconstriction or directly from the biochemical mediators involved in the pathogenesis of fibrotic lung disease.
I believe that the patient's pulmonary hypertension developed as a result of the fibrotic lung disease.
Some features of this case are suggestive of idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis, nonspecific interstitial pneumonia, or desquamative interstitial pneumonia.

The radiographic appearance of extensive ground-glass opacities superimposed on underlying fibrotic changes can indicate the development of a new disease process, such as pulmonary hemorrhage, edema, or infection.
Architectural distortion, traction bronchiectasis, and honeycombing, as seen on the CT scans, are characteristic radiographic features of idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis.
This patient has several clinical features consistent with HPS: oculocutaneous albinism, a bleeding diathesis, and pulmonary fibrosis.
I believe the diagnostic procedure in this case was a surgical lung biopsy, performed with platelet transfusions to prevent potential bleeding complications.
Because of his ethnic background and family history, we were fairly certain that he had HPS, particularly in view of the results of the platelet-aggregation studies.
The dysfunction of melanosomes causes oculocutaneous albinism.
pulmonary symptoms developed in 61% of patients with HPS1, with onset at a mean age of 35 years.
